title: Listening - Part 4: Starting a Side Business
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five people discussing their experiences of starting a small business alongside their full-time jobs

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’ve worked in IT for years, but [21]I started my bespoke furniture business primarily because I needed a creative outlet that involved working with my hands[/21]. I was tired of screens. [26]The most unexpected benefit has been the massive boost in my problem-solving skills at my day job[/26]. Figuring out how to join complex pieces of wood has trained my brain to look at software bugs from a completely different, more structural perspective. I’m much more efficient now. || Speaker 2: I’m a teacher, and [22]I launched my online tutoring platform because I wanted to create a financial safety net for my family[/22]. I wanted that extra security. However, [27]the biggest challenge has been the constant feeling of guilt about not spending enough time with my children during the weekends[/27]. Even when I’m physically with them, my mind is often on my spreadsheets or client emails. It’s a difficult emotional balancing act that I’m still trying to master. || Speaker 3: I’d always been a keen baker, and [23]I started selling my cakes at local markets because my friends kept telling me I was talented enough to turn it into a profession[/23]. I wanted to see if they were right. [28]What I found most rewarding was the incredible sense of community I found among other market traders[/28]. We support each other and share tips, which has been a wonderful antidote to the isolation of my regular office job. It’s given me a whole new social life. || Speaker 4: I’m a journalist, and [24]I started a podcasting consultancy because I wanted to be my own boss and have more control over the projects I took on[/24]. I wanted professional autonomy. [29]The most significant hurdle has been the steep learning curve regarding tax and business legislation[/29]. I’m a writer, not an accountant, and the paperwork has been incredibly overwhelming at times. I’ve had to hire a professional just to keep my head above water. || Speaker 5: I’d been feeling a bit stagnant in my career, so [25]I started a vintage clothing store online because I wanted a new challenge that would force me to learn about marketing and social media[/25]. I wanted to update my skill set. [30]The most rewarding outcome has been the discovery of my own resilience and persistence[/30]. There were months where I sold nothing, but I kept going. That newfound grit has made me much more confident in my main career; I’m no longer afraid of taking risks or facing rejection.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) what primarily motivated each speaker to start their business.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 wanted a creative outlet that involved manual work away from screens.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 wanted to create a financial safety net for her family.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 was encouraged by friends who recognized her talent for baking.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 wanted more professional autonomy and to be her own boss.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 wanted a new challenge to update her marketing and social media skills.

options.A: to prove a point to a former employer
options.C: to improve their long-term financial stability
options.E: to find a non-digital creative release
options.F: to fund an expensive personal hobby
options.G: to have greater choice over their assignments
options.H: to act on the encouragement of others

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions as an outcome of their experience.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 found that his furniture work improved his problem-solving at his day job.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 feels guilty about not spending enough quality time with her children.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 highlights the community and support she found among other traders.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 found the tax and business legislation paperwork overwhelming.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 discovered a new sense of resilience and persistence.

options.A: a decision to leave their full-time job
options.B: the emotional cost of a busy schedule
options.C: an increase in their physical health
options.D: a newfound sense of personal toughness
options.E: a positive transfer of skills to their main career
options.F: the pleasure of seeing their profits grow
options.H: the value of new social connections
